one_line_takeaway: A 2024 survey that catalogs LLM cybersecurity research into a NIST-framework map of defensive uses and a MITRE ATT&CK map of offensive uses, concluding that offensive-risk and post-attack (Respond/Recover) research is comparatively neglected.
key_finding: Research effort is unevenly distributed. For defense (NIST), the collected studies concentrate on the Protect and Detect functions, while Identify, Respond, and especially Recover are sparsely covered (Figure 1) - a gap in post-attack scenarios. For offense (MITRE ATT&CK), studies concentrate on Initial Access, Defense Evasion, and Execution, with little coverage of later-stage tactics (Figure 2). The overarching conclusion: defensive opportunities of LLMs are well explored, but the risks of offensive/adversarial LLM use are comparatively under-examined and need more study.
core_contribution: This is a literature survey (not a system or empirical paper). Its own contribution is a dual taxonomy that organizes the existing research on LLMs in cybersecurity along two axes: (1) DEFENSIVE applications classified through the five functions of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover), and (2) ADVERSARIAL/OFFENSIVE applications classified through MITRE ATT&CK tactics (Reconnaissance, Initial Access, Execution, Defense Evasion, Credential Access, Collection, Command and Control). The paper synthesizes ~40+ prior works into two summary tables (Table 1 defensive, Table 2 adversarial) and two distribution charts (Figure 1, Figure 2), then uses that mapping to identify concrete research gaps. Note: individual systems discussed (PENTESTGPT, PassGPT, SecureFalcon, KARTAL, SecurityLLM/SecurityBERT, CyBERT, shelLM, WormGPT, etc.) are the SURVEYED works being reviewed, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The survey adopts a dual-use framing of LLMs. On the offensive side it treats the LLM as an attacker capability mapped to MITRE ATT&CK: adversaries use LLMs for reconnaissance/victim-data scraping, spear-phishing and phishing-website generation, malware and payload generation, code obfuscation and antivirus/EDR evasion, password guessing (credential access), data collection via iFrame injection, and command-and-control proxying. It also flags dedicated malicious tools (WormGPT, FraudGPT). On the defensive side, LLMs are the defender's tool against conventional cyber threats (vulnerabilities, anomalous logs, phishing sites, insecure code, smart-contract flaws). The paper's central thesis is that offensive risks are comparatively under-studied relative to defensive opportunities.
limitations: As a survey it produces no original evaluation, so all performance claims are inherited from cited works and not independently verified. Coverage is a January 2024 snapshot dominated by pre-2024 arXiv preprints (many 2023), so it predates most frontier-model and agentic-attack work. The paper-count charts are a coarse proxy for research maturity and depend on the (unspecified) collection/inclusion methodology - no stated search strategy, database, or inclusion criteria, so reproducibility and completeness of the corpus are unclear. The NIST/MITRE mapping is applied manually and some entries are debatable (e.g., a red-teaming and a smart-contract-audit study both appear under Detect). No quantitative synthesis or meta-analysis of the reviewed tools' effectiveness is attempted.

datasets_used: None of its own - this is a literature survey with no original experiments. Datasets it MENTIONS from surveyed works include: Los Alamos National Laboratory Cyber Security Dataset (Tuor et al. anomaly detection), SARD and SeVC benchmarks (VulDetect), a 10,000 smart-contract dataset (Chen et al.), FormAI C-code dataset (SecureFalcon), and an IoT cybersecurity dataset (SecurityLLM/SecurityBERT).
quantitative_results: The paper's OWN quantitative output is frequency counts of the reviewed corpus: defensive studies cluster in the Protect and Detect NIST functions with Identify/Respond/Recover sparse (Figure 1); offensive studies cluster in Initial Access, Defense Evasion, and Execution MITRE tactics (Figure 2). Table 1 lists the defensive publications and Table 2 the adversarial publications with year/category/model.

Numbers QUOTED FROM SURVEYED WORKS (not the survey's own measurements): David et al. detect 52 compromised DeFi protocols in LLM smart-contract auditing; Chen et al. trained an LLM on 10,000 smart contracts to evaluate detection of nine vulnerability types (finding frequent false positives); SecurityLLM identifies fourteen types of cyber threats; Chatzoglou et al. report only 3 of 12 antivirus applications detected LLM-generated evasive malware; Sandoval et al. found AI-assisted users introduced security flaws at a rate below ten percent.
baselines_compared: None - a survey has no experimental baselines. It positions itself against prior surveys that focused on LLM safety aspects (refs [1,38,39,49]), arguing those emphasize LLM safety whereas this study focuses specifically on cyberdefense and cyberattack applications.
